column37:
My name is Qabale Haro, a Grade 10 student at Dakabaricha Mixed Day Secondary School. I come from a very humble background. My parents are elderly and do not have a stable source of income, which has made paying school fees extremely difficult. Because of this challenge, some of my siblings were forced to drop out of school. I nearly faced the same fate and almost failed to join secondary school.

Despite these hardships, I have always been determined to succeed in my studies. I work hard and consistently perform well academically. My commitment and good performance attracted the attention of my village elders and the area chief, who came together to support me so that I could continue my education. Their help rescued my dream of staying in school.

My school experience so far has taught me resilience, discipline, and gratitude. Every day in school is an opportunity I value deeply because I know how close I came to losing it. The challenges I have faced have strengthened my desire to work harder, not only to change my own life but also to support my family and inspire other students from difficult backgrounds to believe that education is still possible.
column38:
I should be considered for this scholarship because I am deeply committed to my education and determined to succeed despite serious financial challenges. Coming from a humble background with elderly parents who are unable to pay school fees, continuing my education has not been easy. However, I have remained focused, disciplined, and hardworking in my studies, and I consistently strive to perform well academically.

This scholarship would make a life-changing difference in my education by allowing me to stay in school without constant worry about fees. It would give me the stability and motivation to concentrate fully on learning and achieving my academic goals. My goal is to complete my secondary education, pursue further studies, and use my knowledge and skills to support my family and contribute positively to my community.

By investing in my education, this scholarship would not only support a student who is eager to learn but also help create a future where I can give back and become an example of hope for other students facing similar challenges.

column20:
I come from a very humble background in Moyale Subcounty, Marsabit County—an extremely dry and impoverished region where crops cannot grow and families struggle daily to survive. Our area also faces insecurity due to recurring tribal clashes. Additionally, my family experiences rejection and hostility because we are Christians living in an Islamic-dominated community. These hardships have shaped my life and greatly limited the support available to me.
My father is now 80 years old and is seriously ill. He  cannot get out of bed, and is completely dependent on care. My mother carries an extremely heavy burden as she struggles to provide for the family through small and unstable casual jobs while caring for my sick father, my seven siblings, and three orphaned cousins who live with us because we have no other support system. Due to exhaustion, stress, and poor living conditions, my mother often falls sick, yet she continues to persevere out of necessity. None of my siblings has been able to attend university due to lack of school fees, and several were forced to drop out of school entirely.
Throughout my primary and secondary school education, I was blessed to receive consistent support from our local A.I.C Church in Moyale, which made it possible for me to complete my studies. Unfortunately, due to financial constraints, prolonged drought, and widespread poverty in the area, the church is no longer able to support me at the university level.
I applied for a HELB loan, but the funds have not yet been disbursed to the school account, leaving me stranded. Life at school has been extremely difficult without fees or adequate upkeep. At times, I struggle to meet basic needs, and even calling home to ask for support leaves me deeply worried, as the situation there is equally desperate.
Due to these financial challenges, I was forced to defer a semester. I was also unable to proceed to clinicals because I could not afford to take all the required units, which has caused me to fall behind in syllabus coverage. Even surviving here at school has been difficult, and the uncertainty surrounding my studies has been emotionally and mentally draining.
Despite these challenges, I continue to pray and trust that God will make a way. This education is my only chance to break the cycle of poverty in my family and in the community around me. My aspiration is to become a nurse and to use the skills, knowledge, and resources I gain to care for the less fortunate and serve vulnerable populations. I am fully committed to learning, working hard, and making the most of every opportunity if allowed to continue my studies.
I therefore humbly appeal  for any form of financial assistance or consideration that may help me continue my education. I would be deeply grateful for any support extended to me.
Thank you very much for your time, compassion, and understanding. I remain hopeful that my appeal will be kindly considered.
column21: If I receive support, my main goal this academic year is to fully dedicate myself to my studies and successfully complete all required coursework and clinical training without interruption. I am committed to working consistently, improving my academic performance, and making the most of this opportunity to progress steadily toward completing my program on time. This support would allow me to focus on my education, build the skills and discipline needed for my future career, and move closer to achieving my long-term professional goals.

column37:
Coming from a family where both parents have been chronically ill due to HIV and AIDS, life has not been easy to me. For my primary and junior school, I learnt in our local school where I used to walk daily, some kilometers away. I faced a lot of challenges; school uniform, books and even food were hard to get. My parents, having sold all the parcel of land they had due to sickness, have left with nothing to offer. Not even food for the family.
During weekends, I used to burn charcoal and work on people's shamba just to get some money to buy my school items and help mum buy food. I am only fifteen years old.
Despite all these challenges, I never stopped working hard. I would wake up early in the morning and became consistent in my schoolwork, which managed me to pass my KJSEA exams.

One of the most painful moments was when the time for joining grade 10 came. One week after everybody had joined, I was still at home, parents stranded with nothing to support. My dad, only having 2000/= opted for me to join our local C4 day school. I felt my dreams were shuttered and my hopes were all crushed. I have had a dream of one day becoming a doctor, to help my parents. I could see this dream vanishing from my sight. I had secured a chance at St. Mary's Yalla and that's where I wanted to go and study. I cried but accepted the situation. A day before my joining, my father met a well-wisher who decided to buy our only remaining shamba and add my father some cash to help me study at my dream school.
This was a good idea, but short term. The money could hardly pay half of my first term's fees after buying the requirements. I joined Yalla, one and half weeks after students had joined, but not settled yet. I only paid half of the fee and never bought all the required items. I know so well that all our resources are depleted and if no help comes soon, I may only stay here for one term. I am today in Yalla, but cant concentrate on my studies. The poverty at home keeps on drilling me day by day. I believe that one day, God will see me throuh, and I will accomplish my dream of becoming a doctor
column38:
My education is the only way-through for my survival. Coming from a poverty-stricken family, with chronically ill parents, my education will never be successful without any support. My parents have no resource left. They just sold their last remaining piece of land, which hardly paid for half of my term one fees and the requirements. My education my end in first term, if no support comes through. I still believe in my dream of one day becoming a doctor, a passion I had developed seeing my parents suffering from sickness.
This scholarship will for sure, greatly be an eye opener for me, making me stay in school just like any other children from other background. This scholarship will enable me accomplish me dream and help my parents.

column20:
Our monthly household income of 12,000 shillings is generated by my mother, who works as a local nursery school teacher. This amount usually goes directly toward daily survival and tuition fees for my sister, who is in Form 3 at a boarding secondary school, hence rendering it unavailable to me. This leaves me to heavily depend on HELB upkeep for survival.

Pursuing a trimester degree, which comes with an outstanding fee balance of 33,000 per semester and spending of not less than 20,000 per semester, is impossible because my HELB upkeep is only 22,000 and usually comes in twice a year. This has affected my studies completely because I am not able to sit for my first-semester, first-year exams, as the school does not issue exams to students with outstanding fee balances. I might sit for the exams in August if the situation gets any better or be forced to face the hard reality and defer from school very soon.
column21: If granted support, my primary goal is to achieve academic excellence to stabilize my foundation after a difficult start. Having been barred from exams due to financial constraints, I am determined to bridge this gap and prove my scholastic capability. Beyond the classroom, I am committed to growing "We Run Kakamega," a running squad I co-founded. Athletics is my outlet for resilience, and I dream of building this team into a platform that inspires others. Financial stability will allow me to shift my focus from the stress of outstanding balances to my dual passions: finishing my first-year units with strong grades and leading my squad toward a future of athletic success and community impact.

column37: Lihasi was born in Womulalu, Vihiga County and has lived with the grandmother for most of his early years. His mother is a class 7 drop out who had to leave him home and go to the city to try make ends meet. She has been doing laundry for people whenever called upon and thats how she gets through life. Lihasi had to move to Nairobi following his grandmother's heart illness. They live in Kibera where life has not been easy for him, often sleeping outside because of rent payment, going without food, skipping school because of fees but he has held his head high through it all. He has been back and forth through relatives willing to house him and currently the only stability he knows is the school he is in now of which it won't be for long as the school fees is quite difficult to raise.
column38: Having been from a broken family, Lihasi has not let that come between his studies and he strives to make a difference in his life. He often says "I do not want to live a life like my mum's, I want to live differently and I am hoping studying will take me a step closer to my dreams". Being considered for this scholarship is not just for Lihasi but a voice of hope to a child on the verge of breaking, it is breaking a cycle and lifting him.

column35: My school fees are partially paid, and I have an outstanding balance
column37:
I am a Form Four student who has worked very hard to stay in school despite many challenges. My school journey has not been easy, but it has shaped me into a resilient and determined person. I come from a humble background where my mother, who is currently jobless, is the sole provider for me and my siblings. All of us depend entirely on her, and every school term becomes a difficult season for our family.

My mother has tried her best to keep me in school by going to different bursary distribution centers, but unfortunately, she has often faced discrimination and has not been assisted. Many times, I have been sent home due to school fees arrears, which has affected my learning and emotional well-being. Despite this, I have never given up. I always return to school with the determination to catch up, work harder, and do my best academically.

School means everything to me. It is my safe space and my hope for a better future. Even with frequent interruptions, I have remained focused on my studies because I believe education is the only way I can change my life and support my family in the future. My experience has taught me patience, perseverance, and the value of never giving up, even when the situation feels impossible.
column38:
I believe I should be considered for the Soipan Tuya Foundation scholarship because it represents my only true hope of completing my education without constant fear of being sent home. I am deeply committed to learning and I have shown resilience by continuing with my studies despite financial hardships that could easily have forced me to drop out of school.

This scholarship would make a life-changing difference for me. It would give me the stability and peace of mind to fully focus on my studies, especially now that I am in Form Four, a very critical year for my future. My dream is to perform well, pursue further education and one day be in a position to support my family and give back to my community.

By supporting me, the Soipan Tuya Foundation would not only be helping one student, but an entire family that depends on the hope that education brings. I am determined to make the most of this opportunity, work hard, and prove that with the right support, even students from the most challenging backgrounds can succeed.

column20:
I did my KCSE at St Mary's Viwandani Educational Centre here in Mukuru slums, Nairobi in the year 2015 and managed to get mean grade of C+.

Since I could not afford to join a university. In 2018 I joined college.

Two years later after I joined the Kabete National Polytechnic to pursue a Diploma in Human Resource Management that is when  COVID-19 hit I was not able to proceed with my education having done only two modules meaning two years.(Module 1 and Module 2). I was rendered jobless in a part time job that I was working at. My mother moved to my grandmother's house in the rural area who was bedridden and later died due to complications during the lockdown period

I was forced to abandon my dream of ever stepping back into the halls of education. I had to support my myself, my brother and now my mother who was forced to take care of our one hectare land in the rural area. It has really taken some time to finally even dream of going back to school considering am unemployed and with no educational qualifications to help at least secure a job.

Am currently stuck with no hopes of continuing with my dream of becoming a human resource personnel considering the old CBET will be phased out soon and the dream I once chased with blood and  sweat will be for nothing- I will be merely a college dropout.

Am writing seeking your assistance to help me realise my dream by helping me finish my Diploma Course with only one module left for me to finish, final year.


I have been able to visit the College of Human Resource Management inquiring if it possible to continue with Module 3 considering it the only institution offering the old curriculum so that I can finally acquire a diploma in Human resource management.
column21: That one goal am committed to work towards this academic year if you support me is to finish my module 3 and finally acquire a Diploma in Human Resource Management.

column37:
My name is Gift Nekesa Okumu, a 15-year-old student currently in Grade 10 at St. Theresa’s Girls High School, Kimilili. My educational journey has been shaped by both determination and significant challenges. I am a partial orphan and was raised by my widowed mother, who is a housewife. Due to financial constraints and family responsibilities, my early schooling was in a nearby primary school, where limited resources and home challenges sometimes affected my academic performance. Despite these difficulties, I remained committed to my studies and worked hard to complete primary school.
Joining secondary school marked an important turning point in my life. I chose to attend a boarding school, even though it is financially demanding, because I believe it offers a better learning environment, stability, and time to focus fully on my education compared to day schooling, which previously affected my performance. I am currently pursuing the Social Sciences pathway, as I am passionate about understanding society and using education to improve the lives of my family and community. Beyond academics, I am actively involved in netball and football, which have helped me build discipline and confidence. I am eager to continue developing both my academic abilities and my talents as I work toward a future where I can support my family and give back to society.
column38: This would directly support my commitment to learning and help me overcome financial barriers that threaten my education. I come from a humble background and am a partial orphan raised by a widowed mother who struggles to meet basic needs. Despite these challenges, I have remained determined to pursue my studies and perform well in school, believing that education is the most reliable way to improve my family’s future. This scholarship would allow me to remain in a boarding school environment where I can focus fully on my academics without the constant worry of school fees . It would greatly improve my academic performance and also give me the opportunity to continue developing my talents. I am committed to working hard and making good use of this opportunity.

column37:
Steve is a Grade 10 student who has just joined Daniel Adventist High School, where I was selected to pursue the STEM Pathway after passing his Grade 9 exams well. His journey in education has been marked by both determination and hardship.
Throughout primary school, I struggled due to limited resources and an ailing father who was the sole breadwinner, working daily to provide food for the family. With the support of well-wishers, I managed to attend a nearby day school and successfully completed Grade 9. Sadly, my  father passed away in November last year, just as I was sitting for his final exams.
Now in high school, he reported with only school uniforms, and kind supporters helped  with basic items like used metal boxes. However, none of the school fees have been paid, and  mother, who is caring for nine other children without any source of income, cannot afford to support  due to health issues as well. The well-wishers who assisted during primary school are finding it difficult to continue helping because of the many siblings also in need. Despite these challenges, I  remains committed to education and hopeful that with support, I can excel in the STEM Pathway, achieve my dreams, and one day uplift the family and community.
column38:
I believe I should be considered for this scholarship because of my strong commitment to education and my determination to overcome challenges. Despite facing many hardships; including the loss of my father, who was the sole breadwinner, and the struggles of my mother who now cares for ten children without income, I have remained focused on my studies. With the help of well-wishers, I completed primary school and passed my Grade 9 exams well, earning selection into the STEM Pathway at Daniel Adventist High School.
This opportunity is very important to me because I want to excel in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics, and use my education to create a better future for my family and community. Without financial support, continuing my education is uncertain, as none of my school fees have been paid and my family cannot afford them.
Receiving this scholarship would not only ease the burden on my mother but also allow me to concentrate fully on my studies. It would give me the chance to achieve my academic goals, unlock my potential, and eventually uplift others who face similar struggles.
